op.name: bitwise_and
op.summary: Compute the bit-wise AND of two arrays element-wise.
op.signature: we.bitwise_and(x1, x2, /, out=None, *, where=True, casting='same_kind', order='K', dtype=None, subok=True[, signature])

blocks.text: Compute the bit-wise AND of two arrays element-wise.

Computes the bit-wise AND of the underlying binary representation of
the integers in the input arrays. This ufunc implements the C/Python
operator ``&``.

desc_blocks.text:
Only integer and boolean types are handled.
If ``x1.shape != x2.shape``, they must be broadcastable to a common
shape (which becomes the shape of the output).

items.name: out
items.type: ndarray, None, or tuple of ndarray and None, optional

desc_blocks.text:
A location into which the result is stored. If provided, it must have
a shape that the inputs broadcast to. If not provided or None,
a freshly-allocated array is returned. A tuple (possible only as a
keyword argument) must have length equal to the number of outputs.

This condition is broadcast over the input. At locations where the
condition is True, the `out` array will be set to the ufunc result.
Elsewhere, the `out` array will retain its original value.
Note that if an uninitialized `out` array is created via the default
``out=None``, locations within it where the condition is False will
remain uninitialized.

For other keyword-only arguments, see the
:ref:`ufunc docs <ufuncs.kwargs>`.

Result.
This is a scalar if both `x1` and `x2` are scalars.

blocks.text:
The number 13 is represented by ``00001101``.  Likewise, 17 is
represented by ``00010001``.  The bit-wise AND of 13 and 17 is
therefore ``000000001``, or 1:

blocks.code:
>>> np.bitwise_and([11,7], [4,25])
array([0, 1])
>>> np.bitwise_and(np.array([2,5,255]), np.array([3,14,16]))
array([ 2,  4, 16])
>>> np.bitwise_and([True, True], [False, True])
array([False,  True])

blocks.text:
The ``&`` operator can be used as a shorthand for ``np.bitwise_and`` on
ndarrays.
binary_repr — Return the binary representation of the input number as a string.
